# agentmem Execution Spec — Drift Anchor
*Generated by PlanForge. Not a deliverable — specdrift reference only.*

## Problem
Build agentmem — a 5-layer generic agent memory library — correctly, with postgres+pgvector from day one. Prior attempt failed because agents had too much freedom: they invented their own model names and protocol signatures instead of following the approved design doc.

## Solution
Hybrid stub-generation + reference-implementation strategy. Orchestrator writes all skeleton `.py` files with exact class names, method signatures, and type hints from the 823-line design doc before any agents run. Agents fill in method bodies only — zero architectural freedom. Task descriptions include exact file paths, import paths, class names, and the SQL DDL where relevant.

## Out of Scope
- MCP interface
- SQLite adapter (postgres+pgvector is the reference backend)
- OpenAI / Cohere embedding adapters
- paia-memory migration (blocked until agentmem complete)
- Any feature not explicitly in docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-06-agentmem-design.md

## Success Criteria
- `pytest` passes green with >80% coverage
- All 5 layers implemented: core (models, protocols, domain services), adapters (postgres, ollama, hash, webhook, pg_listen), workers (coordinator, 4 jobs), service (FastAPI), CLI (`am` command)
- `am ingest` and `am retrieve` work end-to-end against a real postgres instance
- No import errors — all modules importable clean

## Key Constraints
- Python 3.12+, psycopg3 (`psycopg[binary]>=3.1`), pgvector, FastAPI, Typer, Pydantic Settings
- TDD mandatory — tests written before implementation bodies
- Class names, method signatures, field names must match the design doc exactly
- Core layer: zero concrete dependencies (protocols only)

## Dissenting Concerns
- Reference implementation has single-point-of-failure risk — if Postgres stub embeds a wrong pattern all agents replicate it
- Validation is semantic not structural — harder to auto-detect drift within correct class names
